topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy brochure for the new city practically mocked me from the coffee table. Liam, my anchor, was trading the familiar scent of our shared apartment for the sterile promise of somewhere completely unknown. I watched him, his smile too bright, too eager, and a cold, tight knot began to coil in my stomach. I spent the next hour meticulously organizing his bookshelf, placing every worn paperback in a precise, almost aggressive alignment. When he finally came home, buzzing with excitement, I didn't offer a hug. Instead, I pointed to a particularly dusty volume. "You know," I said, my voice flat, "this one needs to go. It doesn't fit the new vibe." His enthusiasm deflated instantly, replaced by a flicker of confusion I savored. I made sure to linger by the door when he tried to talk about his new life, my gaze lingering just a second too long, a silent judgment heavy in the air between us.

[story 2]

I found the news tucked inside a forgotten takeout menu—a relocation notice. Across the state. Liam was leaving. The shock didn't register immediately; instead, a slow, deliberate hardening settled over my chest. I walked into his room, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. I didn't cry. Crying felt too weak, too messy for this feeling I was cultivating. "So," I began, leaning against the doorframe, crossing my arms so tightly my knuckles turned white, "are you actually going to leave everything behind? All these ridiculous inside jokes? The routine?" His face went pale. He tried to laugh it off, a brittle sound that didn't reach his eyes. I just watched him, letting the silence stretch until it became unbearable, letting him feel the weight of my disapproval pressing down on him like physical pressure.

[story 3]

The information hit me like a physical blow, sharp and unexpected. Liam was moving. Across the country. A strange, hot surge rose in my throat, a feeling of wanting to dismantle whatever comfortable reality he was building, brick by careful brick. I started cleaning his side of the closet with an almost violent energy, pulling out his favorite, sentimental sweaters and folding them into unnaturally sharp squares. When he finally asked what was wrong, I met his gaze, my expression carefully neutral, almost bored. "It's just... a lot of change," I murmured, my tone laced with an exaggerated sigh. I made sure my hands were steady, my movements economical and precise, communicating volumes through the sheer stillness of my posture. I wanted him to feel the friction, the sudden, unwelcome awareness that his departure had created a noticeable ripple in my carefully ordered world.
